coredata.dc:description: This work introduces a novel thin-film nanocomposite membrane, designed for highly efficient chiral separation, utilizing functionalized Multi-walled Carbon Nantubes (COOH-MWCNTs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Fabricated via interfacial polymerization on a polysulfone support embedded with COOH-MWCNTs, the membrane was evaluated for its ability to separate racemic mixtures of 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine, tryptophan, threonine, tyrosine, and 1-methyltryptophan. The optimized membrane composition was found to be 3 % COOH-MWCNTs and 2 % CQDs in 4 bar operating pressure, feed concentrations at 10 mmol·L−1, and temperature at 35 °C, under which an enantiomeric excess (%ee) was achieved as 99 % for L-tryptophan in the permeate side, the highest among the five tested racemic mixtures. This enhanced separation performance is driven by the synergistic role of COOH-MWCNTs in the support layer and the precise interactions between CQDs and D-tryptophan in the active layer. Additionally, the membrane exhibited excellent long-term stability and antifouling properties, ensuring sustained performance over ten permeation cycles. The membrane's outstanding enantioselectivity, mechanical robustness, and durability represent a significant breakthrough in chiral separation technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work introduces a novel thin-film nanocomposite membrane, designed for highly efficient chiral separation, utilizing functionalized Multi-walled Carbon Nantubes (COOH-MWCNTs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Fabricated via interfacial polymerization on a polysulfone support embedded with COOH-MWCNTs, the membrane was evaluated for its ability to separate racemic mixtures of 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine, tryptophan, threonine, tyrosine, and 1-methyltryptophan. The optimized membrane composition was found to be 3 % COOH-MWCNTs and 2 % CQDs in 4 bar operating pressure, feed concentrations at 10 mmol·L−1, and temperature at 35 °C, under which an enantiomeric excess (%ee) was achieved as 99 % for L-tryptophan in the permeate side, the highest among the five tested racemic mixtures. This enhanced separation performance is driven by the synergistic role of COOH-MWCNTs in the support layer and the precise interactions between CQDs and D-tryptophan in the active layer. Additionally, the membrane exhibited excellent long-term stability and antifouling properties, ensuring sustained performance over ten permeation cycles. The membrane's outstanding enantioselectivity, mechanical robustness, and durability represent a significant breakthrough in chiral separation technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality plays a critical role in the biological activity of molecular fragments such as proteins, amino acids, polysaccharides, and DNA, as well as in pharmaceutically active compounds [1,2]. In many cases, one enantiomer can exhibit desirable pharmacological effects, while its counterpart may be detrimental or inactive. This underscores the necessity of isolating enantiomerically pure isomers in the fields such as pharmaceuticals, food, and agrochemicals [3]. The challenge in separation science arises from the fact that enantiomers are identical in physical properties-boiling and melting points, densities, solubilities, and reactivities—making their separation difficult [4,5]. Traditional methods for chiral separation have utilized polymeric materials such as cellulose-based polysaccharides, crown ethers, calixarenes, cyclodextrins, and mesoporous silica [6,7]. However, the need for more efficient and high-performance separation techniques has led to the development of advanced materials [8]. Recent advancements have introduced carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) with exceptional properties, including high surface areas and good biocompatibility. These materials are characterized by their conjugated π-electron systems and functional groups, which significantly enhance racemic resolution [9–11]. Various CNMs, including graphene derivatives, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have shown promising performance in separation applications through adsorption processes [12,13]. CQDs, in particular, are carbon nanoparticles with surface functionalized cores that offer excellent solubility and further functionalization possibilities due to their oxygen-containing surface groups [14]. Understanding the role of CNTs and CQDs in membrane-based enantioseparation is crucial for optimizing their performance in chiral separation technologies.

Recent literature has explored chiral recognition methods using carbon quantum dots for separating racemic isoleucine, where L-cysteine was employed as a chiral source in a hydrothermal synthesis process. This approach demonstrated significant fluorescence response to the L-isomer while showing minimal response to the D-isomer [15]. Additionally, β-cyclodextrin-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were used in chiral electrochemical sensors, showing selectivity for tryptophan isomers [16]. Zhang et al. developed a liquid quantum dot, (S)-1810-QD, which exhibited high fluorescence activity and selective chiral response, with implications for smart chiral fluidic sensors [17]. The potential of QD composites in polymeric membranes for chiral separation and wastewater treatment has been highlighted in several studies [18–22].

This study aims to advance chiral separation technologies by fabricating a novel chiral membrane, integrating carboxylic functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (COOH-MWCNTs) and CQDs into a thin-film nanocomposite membrane, referred to as TrypCQD-CM. The membrane was synthesized through an interfacial polymerization process using D-tryptophan and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The research evaluates the performance of this membrane in separating various amino acids enantiomers, with a focus on achieving high enantiomeric excess (%ee). The innovative approach of combining CQDs and D-tryptophan which is used as a chiral probe aims to enhance the selectivity and efficiency of chiral separation. This study introduces a novel method for creating a high-performance chiral membrane, providing a promising avenue for advancements in membrane-based separation technologies.

The additive used in the membrane preparation was Polyethylene Glycol (PEG) and Standard Multi-walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNT) with an outer diameter of 2–4 nm, a minimum assay of 60 %, and a length of 0.5–2 μm were obtained from SRL (Sisco Research Laboratory Pvt. Ltd), Mumbai, India, M/s SIGMA-Aldrich supplied Polysulfone (MW = 30,000) for membrane preparation, and 1, 3, 5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid chloride (TMC) (C9H3Cl3O3) for the interfacial polymerization process. The feed components, DL-, D- and L-3, 4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (C9H11NO4), 1-methyltryptophan (C12H14N2O2), Tryptophan (C11H12N2O2), Threonine (C4H9NO3), and Tyrosine (C9H11NO3) were also obtained from M/s SIGMA-Aldrich. n-hexane (C6H14) and N-methyl pyrrolidine (NMP) were obtained from M/s RANKEM chemicals, Gujarat, India and used as solvents.

The functionalization of pristine multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) to COOH-MWCNTs was achieved using an ultrasonic treatment method based on our previous work [23]. In this method, pristine MWCNTs were dispersed in a 1:3 (v/v) solution of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) and sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) and sonicated for 6 h at 30 °C. The ultrasonic treatment facilitates the formation of carboxyl groups (-COOH) on the surface of the MWCNTs, enhancing their dispersibility in polar solvents and compatibility with the polymer matrix. The COOH-MWCNTs were then thoroughly washed with deionized water until neutral pH was achieved, followed by drying at 60 °C under vacuum. Detailed characterization of the COOH-MWCNTs, has been described in the referenced publication [23].

For the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an eco-friendly wet chemical approach was utilized, as established in prior research [24]. In brief, a solution containing 200 mL of 30 % H₂O₂ and 12 g of coal was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 6 h at atmospheric pressure in a microprocessor-based ultrasonicator (Model-Power Sonic 520) within an ice-cold environment to prevent overheating. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and ammonium hydroxide was added to achieve a neutral pH. The product was then filtered using a 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane, and further purification was carried out using dialysis (1 kDa cutoff). Finally, the purified solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator, yielding pure CQDs [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of COOH-MWCNT dispersed polysulfone membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Support membranes were fabricated by dissolving 23 wt% polysulfone (PSf) in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) with 0.15 % polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a pore-forming agent. To enhance the performance of the composite membranes, COOH-MWCNTs were dispersed within the PSf matrix at different concentrations (1 %, 3 %, and 5 % w/v), resulting in membrane samples named as MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3, respectively. The incorporation of COOH-MWCNTs aimed to improve mechanical properties, permeability, and the compatibility of the support layer with the thin-film nanocomposite layer. The COOH-MWCNTs were dispersed in the PSf solution through continuous stirring at 35 °C for 6 h to ensure homogeneous dispersion. After that, the mixture was cast onto a glass plate using a clean glass rod and subjected to dip into the coagulation bath. This led to the formation of a porous support membrane, which was subsequently washed with deionized water to remove residual solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of chiral polyamide layers over the composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The thin chiral polyamide layer was fabricated using the interfacial polymerization (IP) technique over the COOH-MWCNT dispersed PSf membranes, following the established protocols [18]. In this process, an aqueous phase containing a specific concentration of CQDs and 2.0 wt% D-tryptophan was prepared, as outlined in Table 1 . The aqueous solution was subjected to vigorous ultrasonic treatment to ensure complete dissolution and dispersion of D-tryptophan and CQDs. Simultaneously, an organic phase was prepared by dissolving 0.2 wt% trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in n-hexane.

To fabricate the chiral selective layer, the COOH-MWCNT dispersed PSf support membranes were first immersed in an aqueous phase containing a 2.0 wt% solution of D-tryptophan (acting as the chiral selector) and CQDs for 5 min. In this phase, D-tryptophan, which possesses amine and carboxylic groups, adsorbs onto the membrane surface, while the CQDs enhance electron mobility and provide additional functional groups for interaction. Following this step, the membranes were transferred into an organic phase containing 0.2 wt% TMC dissolved in n-hexane for another 5 min. At the interface of the immiscible aqueous (D-tryptophan and CQDs) and organic (TMC) phases, an IP reaction occurs between the amino groups of D-tryptophan in the aqueous phase and the acid chloride groups of TMC in the organic phase. This results in the formation of a cross-linked polyamide network on the membrane surface. The inclusion of CQDs and COOH-MWCNTs in the support layer facilitates additional interactions with the forming polyamide matrix, further enhancing the stability and selectivity of the chiral membrane. After polymerization, the membranes are dried at ambient conditions for 10 min to stabilize the selective layer, followed by washing with deionized water to remove any unreacted monomers and impurities. The resulting chiral membranes, named TrypCQD-CM, synergistically integrate D-tryptophan, CQDs, and COOH-MWCNTs to enhance chiral selectivity and stability. For comparative analysis, three different membranes—MIP (without CQDs and COOH-MWCNTs), MCNT (without CQDs), and MQD (without COOH-MWCNTs)—were also fabricated using the same protocol to evaluate the specific contributions of each component. The detailed concentrations of each monomer used in the IP process are provided in Table 1, while Fig. 1 schematically illustrates the IP process and the formation of the chiral selective layer.

The experimental setup for membrane permeation study involved a sophisticated arrangement of three two-compartment membrane cells, illustrated in Fig. 2 . This apparatus facilitated a comprehensive study of membrane performance under controlled conditions. A variety of membranes, including MIP, MCNT, MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3, were subjected to the experimental protocol, utilizing racemic tyrosine as the primary feed solution. The experimental parameters were set to ensure optimal conditions, comprising of 10 mmol.L−1 feed concentration, pressure maintained at 4 bar, a rate of flow of 25 mL.min−1, and a constant temperature of 35 °C. These conditions were established based on the findings from a series of preliminary experiments, as detailed in our previous publications [18,19]. All membranes underwent rigorous testing for tyrosine enantiomeric separation, with a focus on identifying the top-performing membrane. The membrane demonstrating superior separation efficiency with racemic tyrosine was selected for subsequent experiments involving the chiral resolution of other amino acids 3, 4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA), 1-methyltryptophan, tryptophan, and threonine. This comprehensive approach allowed us to assess the versatility and effectiveness of the developed membranes across a spectrum of chiral compounds, providing valuable insights into their potential applications in diverse separation processes.

The permeation experiment was conducted using three identical membrane pieces, each possessing an area of 20 cm2. These membrane pieces were positioned in the two compartments of three membrane cells connected in series, ensuring that the process flows continuously and sequentially through each unit. This configuration allows for better control and monitoring of the process, as well as the ability to assess any cumulative effects across the three units. It ensures consistent conditions and facilitates comparison between the membranes. The experimental process was initiated by applying a pressure of 4 bar to the circulating racemic solution in the upper section of the membrane cell. During this stage, the racemic mixture engaged with the chiral sites on the surface of the membrane, resulting in specific adsorption. The solution that permeated through the three connected membrane cells was recycled to feed the solution continuously. At regular one-hour intervals, 5 mL samples were systematically collected for further analysis. Subsequently, the collected samples underwent detailed analysis using Ultra High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (UHPLC) equipped with a CHIROBIOTIC®T chiral column (diameter 25 cm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm). Each racemic mixture exhibited distinctive absorption maxima, as outlined in Table S1. The UHPLC chromatogram accurately depicted the true peak area of each isomer, and this information was utilized in conjunction utilizing standard calibration curves to discern the identity of each specific isomer. Eq. (1) was applied for the quantification of the enantiomeric excess (%ee) in the permeate solution, allowing for a quantitative assessment of the chiral separation efficiency achieved during the permeation experiment.

where CD-isomer and CL-isomer represent the concentrations of the D- and L-isomers, respectively, at any given time point [19].

The preliminary structural investigation of TrypCQD-CM membranes (MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3) was conducted using FTIR, as depicted in Fig. 3(a). The modification strategy used in the preparation of these membranes through the IP process was convincingly confirmed by discerning changes in the stretching vibrations of key chemical bonds. For MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3 membranes, peaks observed at 1661 cm−1, 2982 cm−1, and 3417 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO (carbonyl group), C − H (alkane), and N − H (amine) bonds, respectively. Additionally, the vibrational frequency associated with C-H/C-C stretching was attributed to conjugated D-tryptophan. Fig. 3(a) highlighted peaks at 1251 cm−1, 1275 cm−1, 1362 cm−1, and 1562 cm−1, indicating the presence of C–O–C (ether), SO (sulfoxide), O=S=O (sulfonyl), and CC (aromatic ring), respectively. The success of the polymerization reaction was evident from the characteristic peak observed at 1600–1654 cm−1, which corresponds to the CO stretching vibration of the amide (-CONH-) group. This assignment is consistent with the literature, where amide carbonyl stretches are typically observed in the 1600–1650 cm−1 range [26]. The stretching vibrations observed in the range of 1553–1745 cm−1 aligned with overlapping CC stretching and CO stretching vibrations, which can occur due to conjugation effects, as also noted for aromatic compounds and amino acids [27,28]. These results not only validated the modification strategy used during membrane preparation but also provided a comprehensive understanding of the molecular changes occurring at the interface, thereby underpinning the structural integrity and chemical composition of the developed TrypCQD-CM membranes.

The thermal stability of the membranes, namely MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3, was comprehensively assessed through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as illustrated in Fig. 3(b). The optimal thermal treatment involved subjecting the membranes to a heating rate of 10 °C min−1, revealing critical insights into their thermal degradation behaviour. The temperature range of 80 to 110 °C showed a distinct weight loss step which can be attributed to the desorption of water. The TGA profiles of the membranes exhibited remarkable thermal stability, showcasing maximum thermal degradation temperatures of 243 °C, 335 °C, 339 °C, and 381 °C, respectively. The exceptional stability of the MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3 membranes was ascribed to the synergistic interaction facilitated by the polyamide layer formed within the spherical boundary of CQD and the targeted nodular COOH-MWCNT. The heightened percentage of COOH-MWCNT (w/v) led to the encapsulation of the outer surface of CQD with a polyamide linkage, creating a densely packed structure within the hollow CNT skeleton. This robust interaction was primarily attributed to significant pi-pi stacking between CNT and D-tryptophan, manifesting non-covalent electronic movements between –CONH and the arene system of CNTs [18,19]. The observed thermal stability determines the structural integrity and resilience of the developed membranes under elevated temperatures.

The water uptake of MIP, MCNT, MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes were examined using the sessile drop method. The interface energy is correlated with the characteristics of polymeric matrix. For each membrane, we determined the contact angle and related interfacial energy. In Fig. 4(a), the MIP membrane showed a contact angle of 81° as indicated by an arrowhead. It suggests that the surface wettability of the MIP membrane is hydrophobic. Due to superior hydrophobicity, the membrane showed relatively low interfacial energy with higher contact angle value. The presence of TrypCQD-CM imparts more hydrophilicity over the membrane with polyamide layers embedded with a spherical boundary of CQDs. Since they are highly dispersed in water, in addition to the polyamide layer, the membrane surface showed a lower value of dynamic contact angle with higher interfacial surface energy. The best water adhesion properties are demonstrated by MQC2, having a dynamic contact angle of 31°. The reason behind this is that water molecules get gathered over the CQD-D-tryptophan surface with prominent hydrogen bonding and effective interaction between –CONH and water molecules. Therefore, the membrane showed maximum interfacial surface energy of 88 mJ.m−2. In MQC3, the increase in the concentration of COOH-MWCNT may prevent the water adsorption on membrane due to the random settlings of CNT loaded with CQD. This random orientation of CNT deposited over each other has shown a drastic decrease in interface surface energy with increased contact angle [28]. Here, the nodular orientation of CNT over the membrane surface comes into play, whose internal surface cavity was enveloped with CQD-D-tryptophan. This reactive functionality can act as a suitable carrier for water molecules and therefore, the prepared TrypCQD-CM membranes showed hydrophilic character.

The surface charge characteristics of MIP, MCNT, MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3 membranes were evaluated by measuring the zeta-potential, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Zeta-potential measurements were performed using a zeta-potential analyser, which determines the electrophoretic mobility of particles in solution to provide insights into their surface charge and potential. During this measurement, the 1 mM KCl solution was prepared at pH 7 under a controlled temperature of 25 °C. The PSf membrane exhibits a slight negative ζ-potential, whereas MCNT, MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes possess a surface charge at pH value 7 relatively higher negative deviations as evident from Fig. 4(b). These results have reflected the greater negative zeta potential of MQC2. This can be attributed to the electronegative environment of the amide group, which gets deprotonated at basic pH. Therefore, the outer surface of the membrane was embroidered with negative charges. The slight negative zeta potential of the MQD membrane might be because of electron unavailability over its surface [19]. The increase in the percentage of COOH-MWCNT increases more or less charge-inducing agents, but there was a decrease in the negative zeta potential of the MQC3 membrane. This is mainly due to the random overlapping of MWCNT impregnated with CQD/D-tryptophan. Therefore, the conclusion can be drawn from the fact that a negative membrane surface could be effective enough to interact with the targeted isomer with dominating behaviour of the chiral probe embedded on the membrane surface, which exhibited ubiquitous self-isomeric interaction.

The binding energy and composition of unlike elements of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes were measured by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 5 . This characterization technique is based on the penetration ability of an electron beam into only 5 nm of the internal membrane surface. Fig. 5 (a) identified the survey spectrum of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes, wherein it portrayed the occurrence of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), oxygen (O1s) and sulphur (S2p). Fig. 5 (b1, c1, d1, e1) represents the C1s scan of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes, corresponding to the characteristics binding energy at 284.2, 284.5, 284.6, 286.4, 285.4, 287.8 and 288.8 eV for CS, CC, CC, CO, CN, CO and N-C=O/O-C=O respectively. In Fig. 5 (b2, c2, d2, e2), 530–536 eV cover up O1s scan of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes. The binding energy of CO group was observed at 532 eV with high intensity. A peak with a relatively small intensity was observed at 531.8 eV for CO. Most significantly, O1s scan of N-C=O was obtained at a binding shift of 533.8 eV. In Fig. 5 (b3, c3, d3, e3), the S(2p) scan presented CS and O=S=O at 167.6 eV and 168.1 eV respectively. Fig. 5 (c4, d4, e4) represents N1s high resolution scan, which determines NH and N-C=O at 398.7 and 400.1 eV respectively [18–20].

The cross-sectional images reveal the nanostructure in polyamide thin films, indicating asymmetry across the four membranes: MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3 (Fig. 6 (a1, a2, a3, and a4)). The cross-sectional view of MQD membrane highlights pot-like holes on the surface, which, following interfacial polymerization, exhibit a specific layer thickness that evolves from MQC1 to MQC3, further underscoring the formation of nanopores as evident from the images. The MQC1 and MQC2 membranes feature closely packed nodules that form a compact base, creating a relatively smooth interface of polyamide material. Notably, MQC2 presents a rough texture confirming structural irregularities with depressions or voids throughout the active layer. Additionally, in MQC3, the increased concentration of COOH-MWCNT results in the obstruction of membrane pores, leading to alternating features associated with water passage. Atomic Force Microscopic (AFM) analysis was done for topographic determination of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes. The exterior surface roughness of MQD, MQC1, MQC2 and MQC3 membranes were scanned within 3 × 3 μm. The AFM images, as shown in Fig. 6 (b1, b2, b3, and b4), revealed a uniform distribution of nodular membrane morphology. The nodules, which were fused and distributed roughly across the outer area of the membrane surface, possessed interactive forces towards functional moieties attached to the CNT surface. The nodular morphology observed in the AFM images is a result of the continuous polymerization process that takes place over the surface of the membrane, resulting in the formation of a thin and smooth effective layer. The nodules themselves contribute to the asymmetric relationship between the membrane surface and the chiral probe, allowing for efficient enantioselective separation of the racemic mixture.

Preserving the inherent properties of CQDs is essential to maintain their stability and functional interactions within the composite material. To achieve this, non-covalent bonding strategies, such as π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding, are employed to promote the self-assembly of CQDs with other components, including COOH-functionalized MWCNTs. These non-covalent interactions help to retain the original characteristics of CQDs, allowing for stable and functional nanocomposites. They also offer practical advantages during the fabrication process, such as milder conditions and better control over the formation of the composite, thus preserving the integrity of both MWCNTs and CQDs throughout membrane preparation [29,30].

The interaction between MWCNTs in the support layer and CQDs in the active layer was achieved through both physical proximity and chemical affinity. The well-dispersed MWCNTs in the PSf support layer enable a spatial arrangement that brings them into close proximity with the CQDs present in the thin-film active layer. This proximity enhances π-π stacking and hydrophilic interactions, particularly between the carboxyl (-COOH) groups on the CNTs and the hydrophilic moieties on the CQDs, leading to a stable attachment [31–33]. Furthermore, these interactions facilitate electron mobility across the interface between the support and the active layer, enhancing the overall separation performance of the membrane by preferentially capturing the D-form of the racemic mixture [34].

Moreover, chemical reactivity of COOH-functionalized MWCNTs allows for the attachment of CQDs either directly or via intermediate chemical bridges, further enhancing the composite's stability and performance. The non-covalent interaction mechanisms, particularly π-π stacking and hydrophilic bonding, ensure a uniform and controlled integration between the MWCNTs in the support layer and the CQDs in the active layer. This uniform integration is critical for maintaining the functionality and stability of the developed nanocomposite membranes, allowing for precise and effective enantioselective separation in the chiral membrane system [34].

Investigating the procedure of membrane permeation is crucial for understanding the behaviour of membranes under varying process parameters. This study explores the performance of MIP, MCNT, MQD, MQC1, MQC2, and MQC3 membranes in the separation of chiral isomers by systematically altering physicochemical parameters. Employing a two-compartment cell setup as detailed in the previous section, a racemic solution circulated through the membrane under specific conditions: 4 bar of pressure, 10 mmol.L−1 feed concentration, rate of flow of 25 mL.min−1 and at 35 °C. Evaluation of membrane flux and %ee for individual tyrosine isomers after 8 h (Fig. 7(a) and 7(b)) reveals notable variations, particularly in the MQC2 membrane, with observed flux values of 65.87 and 1.08 mmol.m−2.h−1 for L- and D-tyrosine, respectively [19]. Comparatively, MIP membrane, comprising solely the polyamide layer, exhibited the lowest %ee value at 5.94 %, emphasizing the importance of nanoprobe and nanochannels for enhanced interaction with the racemic mixture. The membranes with only COOH-MWCNT (MCNT) and only CQD (MQD) demonstrated moderate %ee values of 54.09 % and 68.36 %, respectively, indicating selective racemic mixture separation. However, the most promising results were achieved when COOH-MWCNT and CQD were combined in the MQC2 membrane, attaining the highest %ee for L-tyrosine at ∼96.8 %. This synergistic effect arises from the complementary properties of the isomer and the chiral probe, optimizing the chiral separation environment. These findings plays the critical role of membrane composition and structure in dictating selectivity and permeation rates. Subsequently, the MQC2 membrane was tested for separating 1-methyltryptophan, DOPA, tryptophan, and threonine, further affirming its superior performance. The substantial influence of membrane surface area on mass transfer rates, particularly towards the L-isomer, highlights the strong interaction of D-tyrosine with the chiral probe [22]. The larger interstitial spaces between polyamide layers, facilitated by tubular CNTs, contribute to higher oxygen functional group fluxes [35,36]. Notably, the gradual decline in %eeL-tyrosine with an increase in COOH-MWCNT concentration emphasizes the intricate interplay between membrane composition and chiral separation efficiency.

Fig. 8 (a1 and b1) presents the time-dependent dynamics of individual isomers during the DL-tyrosine separation process using MQC2 membrane. The membrane flux exhibits a discernible reduction from 64.09 to 46.2 mmol.m−2.h−1 for the L-isomer, whereas, %ee steadily increases over the permeation duration, reaching its peak %ee of 89.03 % observed at the 8-h interval. Concurrently, the D-isomer experiences a gradual decline in membrane flux from 17.97 to 2.79 mmol.m−2.h−1. Notably, prolonging the permeation time to 10 h yields no substantial increase in %eeL-tyrosine, signifying the saturation of the active chiral moieties on the membrane surface [18,19]. This observation aligns with the saturation kinetics inherent to the on the membrane's chiral recognition sites, emphasizing the importance of temporal considerations in achieving optimal separation efficiency. The presented results underscore the intricate interplay between membrane dynamics and chiral separation performance, contributing valuable understanding of the fundamental mechanisms governing the process. The separation influenced by concentration characteristics of a chiral membrane were investigated in an 8 h membrane permeation experiment targeting the enantiomeric separation of racemic tyrosine, Fig. 8(a2 and b2). The highest observed %eeL-tyrosine reached 89.03 % at 10 mmol.L−1 feed concentration. However, as concentration increases, the flux for L-tyrosine decreased from 57.83 to 40.19 mmol.m−2.h−1, while for D-tyrosine, it exhibited an increase from 3.35 to 5.72 mmol.m−2.h−1. Notably, a reduction in %ee to 75.1 % was discerned upon elevating the concentration in the feed to 50 mmol.L−1. This concentration-dependent behaviour underscores the intricate interplay between feed concentration, membrane dynamics, and chiral separation efficiency, providing valuable insights into optimizing conditions for enhanced separation performance. The observed trends highly ght the need for a nuanced understanding of the impact of feed concentration on the chiral separation process.

The membrane performance was systematically investigated under varying transmembrane pressure conditions, significantly influencing both membrane flux and the %ee of L-tyrosine (%eeL-tyrosine) for individual isomers. As illustrated in Fig. 8 (a3 and b3), initially, there was a notable increase in membrane flux for L-tyrosine up to 4 bar, followed by a gradual decrease with further increments in transmembrane pressure. This behaviour is attributed to the selective nature of the membrane surface. Concurrently, the %eeL-tyrosine exhibited a corresponding increase, reaching 89.03 % at 4 bar, followed by a subsequent decline to 82.1 % at 6 bar pressure [22]. This observation underlines the relationship between transmembrane pressure and the chiral separation performance of the membrane, demonstrating the need for careful optimization to achieve optimal separation outcomes. The observed variations in flux for L and D-tyrosine under different pressure conditions can be ascribed to the intricate molecular interactions and transport mechanisms operating within the chiral membrane. With an increase in transmembrane pressure, the driving force for mass transfer through the membrane is augmented. In the case of L-tyrosine, higher pressure leads to an increased flux upto 4 bar, attributed to its enhanced ability to interact readily with the chiral sites on the membrane surface. Conversely, D-tyrosine demonstrates a distinct response. As the pressure rises, D-tyrosine may engage in stronger three-point interactions with chiral sites, simultaneously experiencing heightened steric hindrance, thus reducing its permeability through the selective layer. This behaviour contributes to a decrease in membrane flux for D-tyrosine with increasing pressure. The contrasting behaviours of L and D-tyrosine reflects the importance of considering the specific molecular dynamics and chiral recognition mechanisms influencing their transport within the membrane.

In Fig. 9 (a1 and b1), the impact of permeation time on separating racemic 1-methyltryptophan is depicted. For the L-isomer, membrane flux exhibits an increase to 72.09 mmol.m−2.h−1 at 8 h, while a contrasting decrease to 0.72 mmol.m−2.h−1 is observed for the other isomer. The %ee rises to 98.02 % at 8 h and subsequently experiences a slight decline to 97.11 %. Additionally, marginal changes are noted beyond the optimal time of 8 h for %eeL-1-methyltryptophan. The observed saturation of the active chiral probe contributes to the sustained consistency in %ee, emphasizing the significance of understanding the time-dependent dynamics of chiral separation. Over the 8 h permeation period, the investigation of racemic 1-methyltryptophan separation was conducted with varying feed concentrations, Fig. 9(a2 and b2). The highest %eeL-1-methyltryptophan was found to be 98.01 %. Notably, the saturation of the chiral moiety initiates within chiral recognition sites of the surface of the membrane upon increasing concentration of the feed solution up to 50 mmol.L−1. At this concentration, the L-isomer exhibited a decrease in membrane flux to 51.39 mmol.m−2.h−1 and %eeL-1-methyltryptophan of 78.28 %. Transmembrane pressure was found to be crucial in determining the performance of TFN membranes. Initially, membrane flux for the L-1-methyltryptophan isomer sharply increased up to 4 bar and then gradually decreased, as depicted in Fig. 9(a3 and b3). Conversely, the opposite trend was observed for the other isomer. At a transmembrane pressure of 4 bar, the %eeL-1-methyltryptophan increased to 98.02 % and then declined to 84.19 %, possibly indicating the saturation of chiral sites by the enantiomer. This detailed exploration provides insights into the meticulous relationship of concentration and pressure in the chiral separation process.

The subsequent utilization of the MQC2 membrane aimed at separating racemic DOPA revealed intriguing insights into the time-dependent changes in both flux and %ee, as depicted in Fig. 10 (a1 and b1). Over the course of the separation, the membrane flux gradually decreased from 72.47 to 49.06 mmol.m−2.h−1 for the L-isomer and 19.75 to 2.76 mmol.m−2.h−1 for the D-isomer. This behaviour suggests that the reactive groups of the D-isomer are strategically oriented, providing chiral recognition sites over the membrane surface, resulting in the selective inclusion of this specific isomer [19]. Notably, the design features of the L-isomer exhibited a high %ee value of 96.04 %. This comprehensive exploration sheds light on the dynamic nature of chiral separation and highlights the distinct interactions that contribute to the observed outcomes in DL-DOPA separation. The feed concentration dependent separation dynamics of DL-DOPA was additionally examined. As illustrated in Fig. 10(a2), there is a concentration dependent decline in membrane flux for L-DOPA, while for D-DOPA, a gradual increase in membrane flux is observed with rising feed concentration. Additionally, the %eeL-DOPA (%) exhibited a decline from 96.04 % to 74.21 %, with the maximum value observed at 10 mmol.L−1 feed concentration, as depicted in Fig. 10(b2). The behaviour noted is attributed to the influence of concentration polarization, signifying the saturation of chiral sites because of the self-association among isomers [22,23]. The interplay between feed concentration and membrane performance provides crucial insights into the complex dynamics governing chiral separation in DL-DOPA. Fig. 10(a3 and b3) elucidates the impact of transmembrane pressure on racemic DOPA separation. The results indicate an initial increase in membrane flux for L-DOPA followed by a decrease, whereas the membrane flux for D-DOPA exhibits a decrease followed by an increase beyond 4 bar pressure. At 4 bar, the maximum flux was achieved for L-DOPA, while the minimum flux was observed for D-DOPA. This behaviour is attributed to the dense structure of the inner and outer membrane layers, impeding the diffusion of individual isomers through the membrane matrix, leading to a decrease in flux. Moreover, the heightened collision frequency of the isomers with the membrane matrix contributes to the observed decline in membrane flux. At the optimal pressure, the D-isomer can potentially undergo covalent interaction with a chiral probe located on the surface of the membrane [22]. Gradually, %eeL-DOPA decreases from 96.04 % to 86.18 % beyond 4 bar of transmembrane pressure, indicating the intricate interplay between pressure and chiral separation efficiency.

Fig. 11 (a1 and b1) illustrates the separation of racemic threonine while examining the impact of permeation time on both membrane flux and %ee. The flux values for L-threonine fluctuate between 49.37 and 65.21 mmol.m−2.h−1 over a time of 10 h, while for D-threonine, it varies from 13.40 to 7.20 mmol.m−2.h−1. Initially, the %ee value was approximately 57.3 % after 8 h of permeation, reaching 84.25 %. Subsequently, a gradual decline to 80.1 % was observed beyond 8 h, indicating consistency in %ee at the saturation of the chiral probe. Notably, D-threonine exhibits greater resistance towards permeation, contributing to the observed %ee values and highlighting the selectivity of the chiral membrane for threonine isomers [19]. The influence of feed solution strength on separating DL-threonine is evident, as depicted in Fig. 11 (a2 and b2). The %eeL-threonine was notably achieved at 84.25 % for10 mmol.L−1 concentration of L-threonine during permeation. The concentration dependent behaviour indicates saturation of the isomer over the membrane surface, leading to an increase in flux value for D-threonine to 7.50 mmol.m−2.h−1 [22]. Conversely, a reduction in flux was observed as 41.27 mmol.m−2.h−1 for its antipode with a rise in concentration of the feed. This emphasizes the critical role of feed solution strength in modulating the separation performance of the chiral membrane for threonine isomers. The impact of pressure on membrane performance was investigated by observing variations in flux and %ee during the permeation of racemic threonine through a two-compartment membrane cell, Fig. 11(a3). The results indicate that an increase in pressure leads to an increase in membrane flux for the L isomer of threonine, while the opposite trend is observed for the other antipode. Notably, after reaching 4 bar of transmembrane pressure, there was a slight decrease in the flux value for L-isomer [22]. Fig. 11(b3) illustrates the maximum %ee of 84.25 % achieved at a pressure of 4 bar, identified as the saturation transmembrane pressure. Beyond this optimal pressure, the %ee gradually decreased to 75.11 %, emphasizing the pressure dependent dynamics of enantioselective separation for threonine isomers.

The individual isomeric resolution of tryptophan was systematically investigated concerning permeation time, revealing distinct trends in flux and %ee. For the L-isomer, flux decreased from 67.21 to 47.24 mmol.m−2.h−1, while for the D-isomer, the flux varied from 20.14 to 1.34 mmol.m−2.h−1, as depicted in Fig. 12 (a1 and b1). The exerted flux reached saturation after 8 h of permeation time. Concurrently, eeL-tryptophan (%) demonstrated a substantial increase from 53.89 % to 99.5 %, indicating the preferential adsorption of the D-isomer to the chiral probe on the surface of the membrane. The reactivity of the D-isomer slightly decreased after 8 h of permeation, with consistency observed in eeL-tryptophan (%). Fig. 12 (a2 and b2) showed a variation of flux and eeL-tryptophan (%) of DL-tryptophan with the variation of feed concentration. The membrane flux of L-tryptophan decreased and maxima were observed at 10 mmol.L−1. A rise in the concentration of the feed resulted in a decrease in flux, which can be attributed to factors such as the interplay of the chiral probe with the membrane matrix. The highest eeL-tryptophan (%) of 99.5 % was achieved at 10 mmol.L−1 and further decreases to 79.34 %. The concentration driven increase in flux was observed for D- isomer from 0.15 to 4.73 mmol.m−2.h−1 and for L-tryptophan, decrease in membrane flux was observed from 59.02 to 41.08 mmol.m−2.h−1 on 8 h of permeation. Transmembrane pressure also influences the membrane's effectiveness in separating D- and L- tryptophan. Initially, for L-tryptophan there was an increase in membrane flux till 4 bar and then gradually decreased upon increasing the transmembrane pressure as depicted in Fig. 12(a3). Moreover, L-tryptophan showed a linear increase of %ee with increasing pressure upto 4 bar and %ee was obtained at 99.5 %, Fig. 12(b3). Further, %ee decreases to 94.91 % at 6 bar due to the decrease in membrane selectivity for isomeric resolution through the polymeric membrane [19].

The notable improvement in chiral separation performance observed with the CQD-COOH-MWCNT nanocomposite compared to using only COOH-MWCNT or CQDs alone signifies a significant finding. The inclusion of COOH-MWCNT in the PSf support layer presents two pivotal advantages. Firstly, the porous and nanotubular structure of COOH-MWCNTs offers an increased surface area and greater interstitial space within the support layer. This expanded surface area facilitates enhanced interaction between the feed solution and the support layer, leading to improved mass transfer of chiral isomers to the selective layer. Secondly, the functional groups (-COOH) on the COOH-MWCNT surface can establish stronger interactions with the polyamide selective layer, augmenting its attachment and stability [35]. Consequently, this influences the overall performance of the chiral membrane by enabling more efficient transport of chiral isomers from the support layer to the selective layer. The presence of COOH-MWCNT in the support layer thus indirectly contributes to the enhanced performance of the CQD-COOH-MWCNT nanocomposite.

The separation of chiral isomers through membrane-based separation processes involves intricate interactions between the chiral solutes, the membrane material, and the process conditions. The mechanisms underlying the operation time, feed concentration, and pressure dependencies of flux and selectivity in chiral isomer separation can be comprehended through various factors: over time, membrane-based chiral separation performance can change due to factors such as fouling, cake formation, and membrane aging. These factors can lead to reduced flux and altered selectivity. As chiral solutes accumulate on the membrane surface or within the pores, fouling and cake formation may occur, reducing the effective pore size and altering interactions between the solutes and the membrane. Consequently, flux decreases, and selectivity might change due to altered interactions. The membrane material itself can age or degrade over time, modifying its separation properties, including changes in pore size, surface chemistry, and overall structure that can affect both flux and selectivity [36].

The concentration of chiral solutes in the feed solution significantly impacts the flux and selectivity in chiral separation. At higher feed concentrations, concentration polarization can become more pronounced, leading to a thicker layer of chiral solutes near the membrane surface, reducing the driving force for mass transfer and subsequently decreasing flux. Concentration polarization can also impact selectivity, as the accumulated solutes near the membrane surface might experience altered interactions. The interaction between the chiral solutes and the membrane surface may change at different feed concentrations, resulting in variations in membrane selectivity for different chiral isomers. Pressure plays a crucial role in membrane processes and can affect both flux and selectivity in chiral isomer separation. Higher pressure increases the driving force for mass transfer through the membrane, resulting in higher flux. However, excessively high pressures may result in membrane compaction, altering its pore structure and affecting both flux and selectivity. Increased pressure can change the conformation of the chiral solutes and alter their interaction with the membrane, leading to changes in selectivity. Extreme pressure conditions can deform the membrane structure, impacting both flux and selectivity by altering the membrane's effective pore size and interactions with the chiral solutes. Achieving high selectivity while maintaining reasonable flux rates in chiral isomer separation is a delicate balance. Process optimization, including selecting the appropriate membrane material, designing proper feed conditions, and controlling pressure, is crucial to achieving the desired separation outcomes [37]. Furthermore, monitoring and adjusting operational parameters over time are necessary to ensure consistent and efficient chiral isomer separation.

The results from characterization techniques provide additional insight into the structural changes and performance of the MQC2 membrane during and after the permeation experiments. FTIR analysis (Fig. S1) revealed shifts in characteristic peaks, such as CO stretching and NH bonds, indicating the interaction between the membrane and permeating amino acids. XRD patterns (Fig. S2) confirmed the presence of nanofillers like MWCNTs and CQDs, contributing to the membrane's enhanced crystallinity and stability, which are critical for chiral separation. The membrane lifetime study (Fig. S3) demonstrated the membrane's durability over 10 permeation cycles with minimal flux decline and stable enantiomeric excess, underscoring the effectiveness of backwashing in maintaining performance. Additionally, FESEM images (Fig. S4) showed a thin layer of isomer deposition post-permeation, highlighting the importance of periodic backwashing in preventing fouling. The antifouling properties of the developed membranes were further validated by flux recovery ratios (FRR) as shown in Fig. S5, with MQC2 exhibiting the best antifouling performance due to the hydrophilicity imparted by COOH-MWCNTs and CQDs in the polymer matrix.

Fig. S6 shows typical UHPLC chromatograms of DL-isomer and membrane-treated samples. Table S2 provides retention duration and relative peak area (%) for membrane-treated samples. UHPLC chromatogram of the racemic amino acid feed solution revealed a peak area of almost 50 % for each L and D isomer. It suggests that the estimated value of %ee in the first feed solution of each racemic combination was quite low. It was found that after membrane treatment, the percentage peak area for each isomer could be distinguished well. The L-isomer penetrated the reservoir whereas the D-isomer was strongly adsorbed onto the membrane. As a result, 89.03 % of the desired enantiomers were achieved. In the same way, the HPLC chromatographic plot of additional “substituted” amino acids including DL-DOPA, DL-threonine, and DL-tryptophan in the first feed solution showed the percentage peak area of both isomers is about equal amounts. The enantiomeric DL mixture of both isomers was treated with TFN membrane to break down this barrier, and it was identified that the L-isomer was present in excess in the permeate side relative to the D-isomer based on the findings of the UHPLC study. As a result, Table S2 depicts %ee for L-1-methyltryptophan, L-DOPA, L-threonine, and L-tryptophan were determined to be 98.01, 96.04, 84.25, and 99.5 %, respectively. However, whether the feed solution is treated with a membrane or not, the retention time (min) (Rt), is necessary for the passage of each isomer.

It can be deduced that CNTs and CQDs can be considered as promising nanofillers for designing TFN membranes due to their superior water dispersibility and higher efficiency in entrapping desired molecules within their skeletal matrices. The hydrophilic nature of CQDs can effectively conduct electrons within their ionic nanophase, thus their presence on the membrane surface can serve as bridging interlinks to the CQD/D-tryptophan, which enhances water permeability and enables the separation of individual isomers due to the membrane hydrophilicity [38]. In addition, their dispersion ability acts as a driving force in fabricating the polyamide layer, thus increasing its functionality. The functional groups attached to the boundary layer of CQD can interact specifically or non-specifically with -COOH of MWCNT, reducing chain-chain interactions between polyamide. The hydroxyl groups attached to the reactive site of CQD can increase intermolecular hydrogen bonding across polyamide layers [39]. Incorporating CQDs into TFN membranes can significantly increase the water flux and maintain a steady flux compared to pristine polyamide membranes [40]. It may also enhance nanofiltration performance under organic, polar, and non-polar solvents. The surface hydrophilicity of CQDs can reduce non-specific adsorption due to their bulkiness and D-tryptophan on their spherical surface. Moreover, the addition of CQD can facilitate the migrating power of solvents through the porous membrane surface by providing binding sites [41,42].

The current study unveils the chiral separation mechanism where the D-isomer of each tested amino acid racemic solution consistently adheres to the chiral membrane, while its L-isomer selectively permeates. This behaviour finds support in the calculated %ee values: L-tyrosine (89.03 %), L-DOPA (96.04 %), L-threonine (84.25 %), L-tryptophan (99.5 %), and L-1-methyltryptophan (98.01 %). The chiral separation mechanism hinges on the presence of chiral recognition sites on the membrane surface, employing a synergistic combination of the three-point interaction theory, specific site recognition, and asymmetric space recognition. The three-point interaction theory underscores the formation of a stable conjugate between chiral recognition sites and enantiomers, hindering diffusion rates and prolonging residence time on the membrane. This intricate interaction involves various forces, including hydrogen bonding, π-π interaction, Van der Waals forces, dipole interaction, and electrostatic forces. The specific site recognition principle, intertwined with the three-point interaction theory, accentuates the dynamic interplay between enantiomers and membrane binding sites and forces, leading to diverse selectivity and flux. This interplay facilitates the preferential penetration of target enantiomers through the membrane, while others either remain on the surface or penetrate in limited amounts.

Moreover, the principle of asymmetric space recognition is pivotal, involving the development of a confined chiral channel and asymmetric space by chiral compounds within the membrane. As enantiomers interact with the membrane from the solution, their distinct configurations interact differently with the chiral space, causing discernible differences in diffusion rates. This schemed interplay ultimately results in highly efficient chiral separation, where variance in permeation rates dictates the membrane's separation performance for each enantiomer [43–45]. Regarding MWCNTs and CQDs, their contributions to the chiral separation mechanism lie in modifying the membrane's surface properties, enhancing chiral recognition sites, and creating a conducive environment for interactions between enantiomers and the membrane. The synergy between MWCNTs and CQDs amplifies these effects, thereby improving the overall efficiency and selectivity of the chiral separation process.

In this study a chiral membrane is designed using a TrypCQD-CM nanocomposite, which was systematically evaluated for its performance and explained from the point of host-guest interactions among chiral selectors and probes on the membrane surface. The membrane was assessed for the separation of racemic mixtures considering DOPA, Tryptophan, Threonine, Tyrosine, and 1-methyltryptophan separately. Optimal separation conditions were achieved with 3 % COOH-MWCNT, a pressure of 4 bar, feed concentrations below 10 mmol.L−1, and a temperature of 35 °C, where L-tryptophan showed a maximum %ee of 99 %. Enhanced electron mobility from CQDs to MWCNT skeletal segments significantly improved the capture of the D-isomer of the racemate. The effectiveness of the three-point interaction model confirmed the selective interaction of individual isomers with the chiral probe. The TrypCQD-CM membranes exhibit substantial potential for enantioselectivity, highlighting their promise for the development of robust and efficient chiral separation materials. This advancement opens avenues for the design of novel, mechanically resilient membranes with high efficiency, suitable for applications in the pharmaceutical and related industries.
